items.title: Customer feature vectors in tabular ML
items.scenario: In a churn prediction model, each customer is represented by a vector of numeric features, such as `[monthly_spend, tenure_months, support_tickets]` in R^3. The full dataset is a set of vectors in R^3, and model decisions correspond to geometric structures (like separating hyperplanes) in that vector space.
items.explanation: Thinking of rows as vectors makes it natural to talk about distances between customers, directions that increase churn risk and low-dimensional subspaces that capture most of the variation.

items.title: Text embeddings as high-dimensional vectors
items.scenario: A sentence encoder maps every sentence to a 768-dimensional embedding vector. Similar sentences end up as nearby points in R^768, and directions in the space can correspond to concepts like sentiment, formality or topic.
items.explanation: This embedding space is a vector space: you can add, subtract and scale embeddings while staying in the same space, which underpins operations like interpolation, analogies and vector-based retrieval.

items.title: Images flattened into vectors
items.scenario: A 28x28 grayscale digit image can be flattened into a 784-dimensional vector by listing pixel intensities row by row. A batch of images becomes a set of vectors in R^784.
items.explanation: Treating images as vectors allows you to apply linear methods such as PCA for compression, linear classifiers for recognition and vector-space metrics to compare images.

items.title: Linear constraints as subspaces
items.scenario: In a physics simulation, you restrict motion to a plane through the origin, such as all positions `(x, y, z)` with `z = 0`. This set of positions is a 2D subspace of R^3.
items.explanation: Working in the appropriate subspace simplifies the math and computations: you can express everything in a 2D basis instead of carrying around a third coordinate that is always zero.
